titleStmt.title: Letter from Charles Wyndham Wynne to Alice Katherine Wynne dated 'Monday'
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Charles Wyndham Wynne

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Charles Wyndham Wynne (1895-1917) to his mother, Alice. Charles writes about his leave being postponed while stationed in Donegal, his imminent trip to Bexhill for siege training and some hope that he'll enjoy his time in England upon completion of the course.Charles Wynne served as a captain in the Royal Garrison Artillery during the First World War. His application to the Royal Flying Corps was denied in 1916 due to his poor eyesight. Charles ultimately died of wounds received at Armentieres, June 1917.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Lenan Head Fort Clonmany Co. Donegal Monday Dear Mother, Just a line before
post goes. I am much
disgusted that my leave is
I'm afraid strafed. I rang
up the hotel yesterday but
owing to the line between
here and the Dunree exchange
being in bad order and the
idiocy of whoever Miss Earl or
whoever was at the other end
I didn't get much through.
I may possibly still get from
Tues and in any case hope
to have between the the morning
and evening mail on Thursday.
I am going to Beshill for a
seige course and even when
that's finished I expect I shall
be a good time in England
so may get leave later in
the summer when the weather's
better. Nothing much to report
from here. The two infantry officers
turned out very nice â one
particularly nice but after five
days they both got
orders for the front. Now I must stop for post.
I hope at any rate to be
with you on Thursday
morning. Much love from your
loving son Charles
Mrs a. a. Wynne The Cottage Glendalough Co. Wicklow
